long_reaction_chains.name: Long Reaction Chains
long_reaction_chains.description: Questions involving multiple reaction steps where each step's product becomes the reactant for the next

structural_patterns: Product of one step becomes reactant of next

counting_enumeration.name: Counting/Enumeration
counting_enumeration.description: Questions asking 'how many' compounds satisfy a given property, or enumeration of specific cases

deep_mechanistic_reasoning.name: Deep Mechanistic Reasoning
deep_mechanistic_reasoning.description: Questions requiring understanding of reaction mechanisms beyond surface-level recall - intermediates, transition states, electron flow

comparative_ranking.name: Comparative Ranking
comparative_ranking.description: Questions requiring comparison of properties like acidity, basicity, electrophilicity, nucleophilicity, stability, bond strength, etc.

questions.question_text: A closed vessel contains 10g of an ideal gas X at 300K, which exerts 2 atm pressure. At the same temperature, 80g of another ideal gas Y is added to it and the pressure becomes 6 atm. The ratio of root mean square velocities of X and Y at 300K is
questions.sub_topic: RMS velocity and ideal gas equation
questions.solution_summary: Using PV = nRT, n_X = 2V/RT, n_Y = 4V/RT. Masses: m_X = 10g, m_Y = 80g. M_X = 10/(2V/RT) = 5RT/V, M_Y = 80/(4V/RT) = 20RT/V. V_rms ∝ 1/√M. Ratio V_rms(X):V_rms(Y) = √(M_Y/M_X) = √(20/5) = 2:1.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of ideal gas equation and RMS velocity formula. Standard calculation question.

questions.question_text: At room temperature, disproportionation of an aqueous solution of in situ generated nitrous acid (HNO₂) gives the species
questions.solution_summary: Nitrous acid (HNO₂) disproportionates at room temperature: 3HNO₂ → HNO₃ + 2NO + H₂O. Thus the species are H₃O⁺ (from HNO₃), NO₃⁻, and NO.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of disproportionation reaction and oxidation states of nitrogen.

questions.question_text: Aspartame, an artificial sweetener, is a dipeptide aspartyl phenylalanine methyl ester. The structure of aspartame is
questions.solution_summary: Aspartame is a dipeptide of aspartic acid and phenylalanine methyl ester. The structure contains an amide linkage between the amino group of aspartic acid and the carboxyl group of phenylalanine, with the free carboxyl group of aspartic acid.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Pure recall of the structure of aspartame from the given options.

questions.question_text: Among the following options, select the option in which each complex in Set-I shows geometrical isomerism and the two complexes in Set-II are ionization isomers of each other. [en = H₂NCH₂CH₂NH₂]
questions.solution_summary: Set-I: [Co(NH₃)₃(NO₂)₃] shows fac-mer geometrical isomerism; [Co(en)₂Cl₂] shows cis-trans geometrical isomerism. Set-II: [Co(NH₃)₅Cl]SO₄ and [Co(NH₃)₅(SO₄)]Cl are ionisation isomers.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of geometrical isomerism in octahedral complexes and ionisation isomerism. Comparison across options needed.

questions.question_text: Among the following, the correct statement(s) for electrons in an atom is(are)
questions.sub_topic: Uncertainty principle, Bohr model, Energy levels
questions.solution_summary: Uncertainty principle rules out definite paths (A). Energy in 2s orbital is lower than at infinity (B). Bohr's model: n=1 gives most negative energy (C). Velocity decreases with increasing n (D is incorrect).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of fundamental concepts in atomic structure and Bohr model.

questions.question_text: Reaction of iso-propylbenzene with O₂ followed by treatment with H₃O⁺ forms phenol and a by-product P. Reaction of P with 3 equivalents of Cl₂ gives compound Q. Treatment of Q with Ca(OH)₂ produces compound R and calcium salt S. The correct statement(s) regarding P, Q, R and S is(are)
questions.sub_topic: Cumene to phenol, Chlorination, Chloroform reaction
questions.solution_summary: P = acetone (from cumene oxidation). Acetone + 3Cl₂ → Q = trichloroacetone. Q + Ca(OH)₂ → R = chloroform (CHCl₃) + S = calcium acetate. Chloroform + O₂ in light → phosgene (COCl₂).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Multi-step reaction sequence requiring identification of intermediates and products.

questions.question_text: The option(s) in which at least three molecules follow Octet Rule is(are)
questions.solution_summary: CO₂ (octet), C₂H₄ (octet), NO (odd electron, violates octet), HCl (octet) – 3 molecules follow octet in A. NO₂ (odd electron), O₃ (octet), HCl (octet), H₂SO₄ (octet) – 3 in B. BCl₃ (incomplete octet), NO (odd), NO₂ (odd), H₂SO₄ (octet) – only 1 in C. CO₂ (octet), BCl₃ (incomplete), O₃ (octet), C₂H₄ (octet) – 3 in D.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of Lewis structures and octet rule for various molecules.

questions.question_text: Consider the following volume-temperature (V-T) diagram for the expansion of 5 moles of an ideal monoatomic gas. Considering only P-V work is involved, the total change in enthalpy (in Joule) for the transformation of state in the sequence X→Y→Z is
questions.solution_summary: Using the V-T diagram, the total enthalpy change ΔH = nC_pΔT. Given C_v,m = 12 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, R = 8.3 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, C_p,m = C_v,m + R = 20.3 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹. Calculate ΔT from the diagram and multiply.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires interpreting V-T diagram and applying thermodynamic relations.

questions.question_text: Consider the following reaction, 2H₂(g) + 2NO(g) → N₂(g) + 2H₂O(g) which follows the mechanism given... The order of the reaction is
questions.solution_summary: Rate = k₂[N₂O₂][H₂]. From fast equilibrium: K_eq = [N₂O₂]/[NO]², so [N₂O₂] = K_eq[NO]². Rate = k₂K_eq[NO]²[H₂]. Order = 2 (w.r.t NO) + 1 (w.r.t H₂) = 3.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires applying the steady-state approximation to derive the rate law from the mechanism.

questions.question_text: Complete reaction of acetaldehyde with excess formaldehyde, upon heating with conc. NaOH solution, gives P and Q. Compound P does not give Tollens' test, whereas Q on acidification gives positive Tollens' test. Treatment of P with excess cyclohexanone in the presence of catalytic amount of p-toluenesulfonic acid (PTSA) gives product R. Sum of the number of methylene groups (-CH₂-) and oxygen atoms in R is ______.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldol and Cannizzaro Reactions
questions.solution_summary: Acetaldehyde + formaldehyde → P (pentacrythritol, no Tollens) + Q (formate, Tollens). P + cyclohexanone → acetal (R). R has 4 methylene groups from cyclohexanone rings and 4 oxygen atoms (two acetals), sum = 8.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identifying products of crossed aldol/Cannizzaro and then acetal formation with cyclohexanone.

questions.question_text: Among V(CO)₆, Cr(CO)₅, Cu(CO)₃, Mn(CO)₅, Fe(CO)₅, [Co(CO)₃]³⁻, [Cr(CO)₄]⁴⁻, and Ir(CO)₃ the total number of species isoelectronic with Ni(CO)₄ is ______. [Given, atomic number: V=23, Cr=24, Mn=25, Fe=26, Co=27, Ni=28, Cu=29, Ir=77]
questions.solution_summary: Ni(CO)₄ has Ni(0) with electron count: Ni(28) + 4×2 = 36 electrons. Count electrons for each: V(CO)₆: 23+12=35; Cr(CO)₅: 24+10=34; Cu(CO)₃: 29+6=35; Mn(CO)₅: 25+10=35; Fe(CO)₅: 26+10=36 (yes); [Co(CO)₃]³⁻: 27+6+3=36 (yes); [Cr(CO)₄]⁴⁻: 24+8+4=36 (yes); Ir(CO)₃: 77+6=83. Total 3? Wait: Fe(CO)₅, [Co(CO)₃]³⁻, [Cr(CO)₄]⁴⁻ = 3. Also V(CO)₆? 35 no. Mn(CO)₅? 35 no. So total = 3.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires electron counting in metal carbonyls to identify isoelectronic species.

questions.question_text: In the following reaction sequence, the major product P is formed. Glycerol reacts completely with excess P in the presence of an acid catalyst to form Q. Reaction of Q with excess NaOH followed by treatment with CaCl₂ yields Ca-soap R, quantitatively. Starting with one mole of Q, the amount of R produced in gram is
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Reaction Sequence & Soap Formation
questions.solution_summary: P is a fatty acid (e.g., stearic acid). Glycerol + 3P → Q (triglyceride). Q + NaOH → sodium soap. Sodium soap + CaCl₂ → calcium soap (R). Molar mass of calcium soap = (3×fatty acid salt + Ca) - 2Na. Calculate from given atomic weights.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of esterification, saponification, and stoichiometric calculation.

questions.question_text: Among the following complexes, the total number of diamagnetic species is [Mn(NH₃)₆]³⁺, [MnCl₆]³⁻, [FeF₆]³⁻, [CoF₆]³⁻, [Fe(NH₃)₆]³⁺, and [Co(en)₃]³⁺
questions.solution_summary: Mn³⁺ (d⁴): [Mn(NH₃)₆]³⁺ (NH₃ strong field → low spin, t₂g⁴ → 2 unpaired) paramagnetic; [MnCl₆]³⁻ (Cl⁻ weak field → high spin, 4 unpaired) paramagnetic. Fe³⁺ (d⁵): [FeF₆]³⁻ (F⁻ weak → 5 unpaired) paramagnetic; [Fe(NH₃)₆]³⁺ (NH₃ strong → low spin, t₂g⁵ → 1 unpaired) paramagnetic. Co³⁺ (d⁶): [CoF₆]³⁻ (F⁻ weak → 4 unpaired) paramagnetic; [Co(en)₃]³⁺ (en strong → low spin, t₂g⁶ → 0 unpaired) diamagnetic. Total diamagnetic = 1? Wait check: [Co(en)₃]³⁺ is diamagnetic. Also [Fe(CN)₆]³⁻ would be, but not given. So only 1? Let me recount: [Mn(NH₃)₆]³⁺: Mn³⁺ d⁴, low spin (t₂g⁴), 2 unpaired = paramagnetic. [Co(en)₃]³⁺: Co³⁺ d⁶, low spin (t₂g⁶), 0 unpaired = diamagnetic. So only 1. But there may be [Fe(CN)₆]³⁻ not listed. So total = 1.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires determining d-electron configurations and pairing based on ligand field strength.

questions.question_text: In a conductometric titration, small volume of titrant of higher concentration is added stepwise to a larger volume of titrate... The limiting ionic conductivity values for different ions are given. Match each entry in List-I with appropriate entry in List-II.
questions.solution_summary: Based on Λ₀ values of ions (Ag⁺ 6.2, K⁺ 7.4, Na⁺ 5.0, H⁺ 35.0, NO₃⁻ 7.2, Cl⁻ 7.6, SO₄²⁻ 16.0, OH⁻ 19.9, CH₃COO⁻ 4.1), the conductance curves can be predicted. H⁺ and OH⁻ have highest mobility causing characteristic minima/maxima.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of conductometric titration curves and limiting ionic conductivity values.

questions.question_text: Based on VSEPR model, match the xenon compounds given in List-I with the corresponding geometries and the number of lone pairs on xenon given in List-II and choose the correct option.
questions.sub_topic: Xenon compounds, VSEPR geometry, Lone pairs
questions.solution_summary: XeF₂: linear, 3 lone pairs (sp³d) → 5. XeF₄: square planar, 2 lone pairs (sp³d²) → 3. XeO₃: pyramidal, 1 lone pair (sp³) → 2. XeO₃F₂: trigonal bipyramidal, 0 lone pairs → 4.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of VSEPR structures and lone pair counting for xenon compounds.

questions.question_text: List-I contains various reaction sequences and List-II contains the possible products. Match each entry in List-I with the appropriate entry in List-II and choose the correct option.
questions.solution_summary: Various organic reactions sequences involving named reactions and functional group transformations.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of products from multi-step reaction sequences.

questions.question_text: List-I contains various reaction sequences and List-II contains different phenolic compounds. Match each entry in List-I with the appropriate entry in List-II and choose the correct option.
questions.solution_summary: Various phenol synthesis reactions including Reimer-Tiemann, Kolbe's, nitration, bromination.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of phenolic products from reaction sequences.

questions.question_text: According to Bohr's model, the highest kinetic energy is associated with the electron in the
questions.solution_summary: Kinetic energy ∝ Z²/n². Higher Z and lower n gives higher KE. First orbit of He⁺ (Z=2, n=1) gives KE ∝ 4. First orbit of H (Z=1) gives 1. Second orbit of He⁺ gives 4/4=1. Second orbit of Li²⁺ gives 9/4=2.25. Highest is He⁺ first orbit (Z=2, n=1).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of Bohr's model and kinetic energy relation.

questions.question_text: In a metal deficient oxide sample, MₓY₂O₄ (M and Y are metals), M is present in both +2 and +3 oxidation states and Y is in +3 oxidation state. If the fraction of M²⁺ ions present in M is 1/3, the value of x is
questions.solution_summary: Let fraction of M²⁺ = 1/3, M³⁺ = 2/3. Average oxidation state of M = (1/3)(+2) + (2/3)(+3) = 8/3. Total charge: x(8/3) + 2(+3) + 4(-2) = 0. 8x/3 + 6 - 8 = 0. 8x/3 = 2. x = 6/8 = 0.75.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires charge balance calculation using oxidation states.

questions.question_text: In the following reaction sequence, the major product Q is
questions.solution_summary: Reaction sequence involving dehydrohalogenation, dehydration, and substitution reactions leading to the major product Q.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of reaction sequences and major product formation.

questions.question_text: The species formed on fluorination of phosphorus pentachloride in a polar organic solvent are
questions.solution_summary: PCl₅ + F₂ → [PF₄]⁺ and [PF₆]⁻, and [PCl₄]⁺ and [PF₆]⁻. In polar solvents, PCl₅ ionises to [PCl₄]⁺[PCl₆]⁻, and fluorination replaces Cl with F.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of PCl₅ structure and its reactions with fluorine.

questions.question_text: An aqueous solution of hydrazine (N₂H₄) is electrochemically oxidized by O₂, thereby releasing chemical energy in the form of electrical energy. One of the products generated from the electrochemical reaction is N₂(g). Choose the correct statement(s) about the above process
questions.solution_summary: Anode: N₂H₄ + 4OH⁻ → N₂ + 4H₂O + 4e⁻ (A correct). Cathode: O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ (C correct). B incorrect (N₂H₄ at anode). D incorrect (no NOₓ, only N₂).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of half-cell reactions in a fuel cell.

questions.question_text: The option(s) with correct sequence of reagents for the conversion of P to Q is(are)
questions.solution_summary: The conversion requires selective reduction of alkyne (Lindlar's catalyst, H₂), hydrolysis, reduction (NaBH₄), and diazotisation (SnCl₂/HCl) in the correct sequence.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of reagent selectivity and sequence of reactions for functional group transformations.

questions.question_text: The compound(s) having peroxide linkage is(are)
questions.solution_summary: H₂S₂O₈ (peroxodisulphuric acid) has peroxide linkage (-O-O-). H₂SO₅ (peroxomonosulphuric acid / Caro's acid) has peroxide linkage. H₂S₂O₇ (pyrosulphuric acid) has -O- linkage (not peroxide). H₂S₂O₅ (disulphurous acid) has no peroxide linkage.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of structures of oxoacids of sulphur and identification of peroxide bond.

questions.question_text: To form a complete monolayer of acetic acid on 1g of charcoal, 100 mL of 0.5 M acetic acid was used. Some of the acetic acid remained unadsorbed. To neutralize the unadsorbed acetic acid, 40 mL of 1 M NaOH solution was required. If each molecule of acetic acid occupies P × 10⁻²³ m² surface area on charcoal, the value of P is [Use: Surface area of charcoal = 1.5×10² m² g⁻¹; Avogadro's number (N_A) = 6.0×10²³ mol⁻¹]
questions.solution_summary: Moles of acetic acid initially = 0.1 L × 0.5 M = 0.05 mol. Unadsorbed = moles of NaOH used = 0.04 L × 1 M = 0.04 mol. Adsorbed = 0.05 - 0.04 = 0.01 mol. Number of molecules = 0.01 × 6.0×10²³ = 6.0×10²¹. Area occupied = 1.5×10² m². Area per molecule = 1.5×10² / 6.0×10²¹ = 2.5×10⁻²⁰ m² = 25×10⁻²³ m². P = 25. Wait check: 1.5×10² / 6.0×10²¹ = 2.5×10⁻²⁰. In P×10⁻²³, P = 250? 2.5×10⁻²⁰ = 250×10⁻²³. So P = 250? Let me recalc: Surface area per molecule = area / number = (1.5×10²) / (6.0×10²¹) = 2.5×10⁻²⁰ m² = 2.5×10⁻²⁰ / 10⁻²³ = 2.5×10³ × 10⁻²³ = 2500×10⁻²³. Hmm let me be careful: 2.5×10⁻²⁰ = 2.5×10³ × 10⁻²³ = 2500×10⁻²³. So P = 2500? Wait 2.5×10⁻²⁰ = 250×10⁻²² = 2500×10⁻²³. Yes P = 2500. But the answer expected is 2.5? Let me recalc: 1.5×10² = 150 m². 150 / (6.0×10²¹) = 2.5×10⁻²⁰ m². In P×10⁻²³, 2.5×10⁻²⁰ = 250×10⁻²² = 2500×10⁻²³? No: 10⁻²⁰ = 10³ × 10⁻²³ = 1000×10⁻²³. So 2.5×10⁻²⁰ = 2.5×1000×10⁻²³ = 2500×10⁻²³. So P = 2500. But I think the answer is 2.5? Actually 150 m² per gram, so 150 / (6×10²¹) = 2.5×10⁻²⁰ = 25×10⁻²¹ = 250×10⁻²² = 2500×10⁻²³. So P = 2500.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires stoichiometric calculation of adsorption and surface area per molecule.

questions.question_text: Vessel-1 contains w₂ g of a non-volatile solute X dissolved in w₁ g of water. Vessel-2 contains w₂ g of another non-volatile solute Y dissolved in w₁ g of water. Both the vessels are at the same temperature and pressure. The molar mass of X is 80% of that of Y. The van't Hoff factor for X is 1.2 times of that of Y for their respective concentrations. The elevation of boiling point for solution in Vessel-1 is % of the solution in Vessel-2.
questions.sub_topic: Elevation of boiling point, van't Hoff factor
questions.solution_summary: ΔT_b = i K_b m. For X: M_X = 0.8 M_Y, i_X = 1.2 i_Y. m_X = w₂/(M_X × w₁/1000), m_Y = w₂/(M_Y × w₁/1000). ΔT_b(X) / ΔT_b(Y) = (i_X m_X) / (i_Y m_Y) = (1.2)(M_Y / 0.8 M_Y) = 1.5 = 150%. So ΔT_b(X) is 150% of ΔT_b(Y). Wait question asks
meaning X is what % of Y? If ratio is 1.5, then X is 150% of Y. But the answer might be 150? Let me check: ΔT_b(X) / ΔT_b(Y) = i_X/i_Y × M_Y/M_X = 1.2 × 1/0.8 = 1.5 = 150%. So X is 150% of Y.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of boiling point elevation formula with van't Hoff factor.

questions.question_text: For a double strand DNA, one strand is given below: [Image]. The amount of energy required to split the double strand DNA into two single strands is kcal mol⁻¹. [Given: Average energy per H-bond for A-T base pair = 1.0 kcal mol⁻¹, G-C base pair = 1.5 kcal mol⁻¹, and A-U base pair = 1.25 kcal mol⁻¹. Ignore electrostatic repulsion between the phosphate groups.]
questions.solution_summary: Count the number of A-T and G-C base pairs in the given DNA strand. A-T has 2 H-bonds (1.0 kcal each = 2.0 kcal per pair). G-C has 3 H-bonds (1.5 kcal each = 4.5 kcal per pair). Sum the energy for all base pairs to get total energy.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires counting base pairs and calculating H-bond energy.

questions.question_text: A sample initially contains only U-238 isotope of uranium. With time, some of the U-238 radioactively decays into Pb-206 while the rest of it remains undistinguished. When the age of the sample is P × 10⁸ years, the ratio of mass of Pb-206 to that of U-238 in the sample is found to be 7. The value of P is [Given: Half-life of U-238 is 4.5×10⁹ years; logₑ2 = 0.693]

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of radioactive decay kinetics and mass ratio calculations.

questions.question_text: Among [Co(CN)₄]⁴⁻, [Co(CO)₃(NO)], XeF₄, [PCl₄]⁺, [PdCl₄]²⁻, [ICl₄]⁻, [Cu(CN)₄]³⁻, and P₄ the total number of species with tetrahedral geometry is
questions.solution_summary: [Co(CN)₄]⁴⁻: Co²⁺ d⁷, CN⁻ strong field → low spin d⁷ → sp³ tetrahedral. [Co(CO)₃(NO)]: 18-electron rule → tetrahedral. [PCl₄]⁺: P sp³ → tetrahedral. [PdCl₄]²⁻: Pd²⁺ d⁸ → square planar (not tetrahedral). [ICl₄]⁻: ICl₄⁻ has 4 bond pairs + 2 lone pairs → square planar. [Cu(CN)₄]³⁻: Cu⁺ d¹⁰ → sp³ tetrahedral. XeF₄: square planar. P₄: tetrahedral. Total tetrahedral: [Co(CN)₄]⁴⁻, [Co(CO)₃(NO)], [PCl₄]⁺, [Cu(CN)₄]³⁻, P₄ = 5. Wait check: [PdCl₄]²⁻ is square planar, not tetrahedral. [ICl₄]⁻ square planar. XeF₄ square planar. So tetrahedral: [Co(CN)₄]⁴⁻, [PCl₄]⁺, [Cu(CN)₄]³⁻, P₄ = 4? Also [Co(CO)₃(NO)]: Co(0), 27+6+2=35? Let me check electron count. [Co(CO)₃(NO)]: Co(0) 27, CO 6, NO 3 (as 3-electron donor) = 36 electrons → tetrahedral. So yes: [Co(CN)₄]⁴⁻, [Co(CO)₃(NO)], [PCl₄]⁺, [Cu(CN)₄]³⁻, P₄ = 5.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires determining geometry of various complexes using VSEPR and hybridisation concepts.

questions.question_text: An organic compound P having molecular formula C₆H₆O₃ gives ferric chloride test and does not have intramolecular hydrogen bond. The compound P reacts with 3 equivalents of NH₂OH to produce oxime Q. Treatment of P with excess methyl iodide in the presence of KOH produces compound R as the major product. Reaction of R with excess iso-butylmagnesium bromide followed by treatment with H₃O⁺ gives compound S as the major product. The total number of methyl (-CH₃) group(s) in compound S is
questions.solution_summary: P is phloroglucinol (1,3,5-trihydroxybenzene, C₆H₆O₃). Ferric chloride test positive (phenol). No intramolecular H-bond. 3 equivalents of NH₂OH → trioxime (Q). Methylation with excess CH₃I/KOH → trimethyl ether (R). Grignard: excess iso-butylmagnesium bromide → addition to benzene ring? Actually phloroglucinol trimethyl ether + excess Grignard → after hydrolysis gives 1,3,5-triisobutyl-2,4,6-trimethoxybenzene? Count methyl groups: 3 methoxy (3 CH₃) + 3 isobutyl groups (each has 1 CH + 2 CH₃? Isobutyl is -CH₂-CH(CH₃)₂, so 2 CH₃ per group = 6 CH₃ from isobutyl) + 3 methoxy CH₃ = total 9? Wait iso-butyl is -CH₂-CH(CH₃)₂, so each has 2 CH₃ groups. 3 × 2 = 6. Methoxy groups: 3 × 1 = 3. Total = 9 methyl groups. But let me check: the question might be simpler. Actually R is methylated phloroglucinol. Grignard with excess iso-butylMgBr adds to the aromatic ring? No, it's a phenol ether, so Grignard doesn't add. I think the product S might be 1,3,5-triisobutylbenzene with 3 methoxy groups. Count methyl groups: each isobutyl has 2 CH₃, total 6. So answer is 6.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of phloroglucinol and subsequent transformations to count methyl groups.

questions.question_text: An organic compound P with molecular formula C₉H₁₈O₂ decolorizes bromine water and also shows positive iodoform test. P on ozonolysis followed by treatment with H₂O₂ gives Q and R. While compound Q shows positive iodoform test, compound R does not give positive iodoform test. Q and R on oxidation with pyridinium chlorochromate (PCC) followed by heating give S and T, respectively. Both S and T show positive iodoform test. Sum of number of oxygen atoms in S and T is
questions.solution_summary: P is an unsaturated ketone (decolorizes Br₂, iodoform positive). Ozonolysis with H₂O₂ gives carboxylic acids Q and R. Q gives iodoform (methyl ketone or alcohol), R does not. PCC oxidation of Q and R gives S and T, both iodoform positive. Q and R must have CH₃CO- group. Sum of oxygen atoms in S and T = 4 (two COOH groups) + 2 (two carbonyl groups) = 6.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of products from ozonolysis and oxidation sequences.

questions.question_text: Complete copolymerization of 500 moles of Q and 500 moles of R gives one mole of a single acyclic copolymer U. [Given, atomic mass: H=1, C=12, O=16] The molecular weight of U is
questions.correct_answer: Based on Q and R structures
questions.solution_summary: Q and R are from ozonolysis products. If Q and R are dicarboxylic acids, their copolymerisation gives a polyester. Molecular weight = (MW of Q + MW of R - 2×18) × 500. Calculate based on structures.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires calculation of molecular weight of copolymer from monomers.

questions.question_text: When potassium iodide is added to an aqueous solution of potassium ferricyanide, a reversible reaction is observed in which a complex P is formed. In a strong acidic medium, the equilibrium shifts completely towards P. Addition of zinc chloride to P in a slightly acidic medium results in a sparingly soluble complex Q. The number of moles of potassium iodide required to produce two moles of P is
questions.solution_summary: Potassium ferricyanide [Fe(CN)₆]³⁻ reacts with KI to form complex P. [Fe(CN)₆]³⁻ + 2I⁻ → [Fe(CN)₆I₂]⁵⁻? Actually ferricyanide + iodide gives ferrocyanide + iodine? No, it forms a mixed complex. The reaction: [Fe(CN)₆]³⁻ + 2I⁻ → [Fe(CN)₄I₂]³⁻? Need to determine stoichiometry. For two moles of P, 6 moles of KI are required (each P requires 3 I⁻?).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires stoichiometric calculation from the reaction stoichiometry.

questions.question_text: When potassium iodide is added to an aqueous solution of potassium ferricyanide, a reversible reaction is observed in which a complex P is formed. In a strong acidic medium, the equilibrium shifts completely towards P. Addition of zinc chloride to P in a slightly acidic medium results in a sparingly soluble complex Q. The number of zinc ions present in the molecular formula of Q is
questions.solution_summary: ZnCl₂ reacts with complex P to form a sparingly soluble complex Q. Zn²⁺ forms a complex with the ligands. The molecular formula of Q contains one Zn²⁺ ion.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of the number of zinc ions in the complex Q.
